talk_metadata.talk_title: Dendritic cell metabolism and therapeutic rewiring
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we can improve their metabolic and mitochondria fitness"
Speaker said: "adoptive transfer of CDC1 with better mitochondria fitness shows greatly enhanced ability to slow down tumor growth"
Speaker said: "we have not looked at the metastatic tumor system yet"

key_data_gaps: No in vivo human data presented for DC metabolic reprogramming
Limited data on metastatic tumor models

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify and isolate mitochondrial‑high conventional dendritic cells (cDC1) in human tumors to enable adoptive DC therapy.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "we can isolate CDC1 that are marked with higher mitochondria activity and compare that with total CDC1... the CDC1 with better mitochondria fitness shows greatly enhanced ability to slow down tumor growth"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can predict mitochondrial fitness signatures from single‑cell RNA‑seq and guide selection of optimal DC subsets for therapy.

crispro_opportunity.description: Leverage single‑cell CRISPR screening data to discover metabolic enzymes that regulate DC function, providing novel targets beyond existing small‑molecule pipelines.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker described a single‑cell CRISPR screen of 200 metabolic enzymes that identified modules influencing progenitor‑exhausted versus terminal T cell states.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO’s computational spine can integrate CRISPR perturbation effects with metabolic pathway models to prioritize druggable enzymes.

unresolved_questions: How can the mitochondrial‑high DC phenotype be induced in vivo without ex‑vivo isolation?
What are the signals that repress OPA1 expression in tumor‑associated DCs?

talk_metadata.talk_title: BACH1‑driven transcriptional plasticity and immune evasion in triple‑negative breast cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "BACH1 knockout can really increase amount of CD8 T cell infiltrate into a tumor"
Speaker said: "BACH1 activity really predicts response rate in TNBC in a pembrolizumab and chemo arm"
Speaker said: "we have not looked at the metastatic tumor system yet"

key_data_gaps: Limited clinical validation of BACH1 as a therapeutic target
No data on safety of systemic BACH1 inhibition

crispro_opportunity.description: Target BACH1‑driven transcriptional program to reverse T‑cell exclusion and improve checkpoint inhibitor response in TNBC.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "BACH1 knockout can really increase amount of CD8 T cell infiltrate into a tumor" and "BACH1 activity really predicts response rate in TNBC in a pembrolizumab and chemo arm"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model BACH1‑controlled gene networks and predict small‑molecule or degrader candidates that disrupt the BACH1 transcriptional hub.

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop a BACH1‑activity signature to stratify patients likely to be non‑responders to immunotherapy.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker used a gene‑set (HRO) derived from BACH1 activity to predict T‑cell exclusion and non‑response in I‑SPY and other clinical cohorts.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate the HRO signature with existing transcriptomic datasets to generate a predictive classifier for trial enrollment.

unresolved_questions: What additional stressors besides hypoxia induce BACH1 in tumor cells?
Can pharmacologic BACH1 inhibition be combined with other immunomodulators without inducing compensatory resistance?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Temporal dynamics of PD‑1 blockade and targeting CD22 to rescue exhausted T cells
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "long‑term loss of PD‑1 signals is not always an advantage for these cells"
Speaker said: "a drug holiday allows for sufficient rest for these cells to mount a new reinvigoration response"
Speaker said: "CD22 is a potent negative regulatory molecule on progenitor exhausted T cells"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Continuous PD‑1 blockade erodes progenitor‑exhausted (Tpex) cells and skews differentiation toward terminally exhausted cells

trial_dilution_risk.trial_name_or_nct: Preclinical LCMV chronic infection model (PD‑1 regimen study)
trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: PD‑1 checkpoint inhibition in chronic antigen exposure
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Continuous dosing does not account for T cell exhaustion dynamics, leading to over‑stimulation and loss of Tpex pool
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Absence of serial Tpex monitoring (e.g., Ly108+ CD63+ frequency) to guide dosing intervals
trial_dilution_risk.static_vs_dynamic_failure: No on‑treatment assessment of progenitor exhaustion state

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: Standard anti‑PD‑1 IgG antibodies with long serum and cell‑surface half‑life
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker highlighted that the cell‑surface bound half‑life of nivolumab is ~6 months, limiting ability to implement drug holidays
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Indefinite continuous PD‑1 blockade without scheduled drug holidays may diminish therapeutic benefit

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that continuous PD‑1 blockade yields a peak of T cell reinvigoration then returns to baseline, yet concluded that "long‑term loss of PD‑1 signals is not always an advantage" – contradictory to the notion that sustained blockade should maintain benefit

key_data_gaps: No clinical trial data confirming drug‑holiday benefit in cancer patients
Limited human data on CD22 expression on exhausted T cells

crispro_opportunity.description: Design clinical trials with intermittent PD‑1 dosing schedules (drug holidays) to preserve Tpex cells and improve durability of response.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "a drug holiday allows for sufficient rest for these cells to mount a new reinvigoration response"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate T cell dynamics under different dosing regimens to predict optimal on/off intervals.

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop bispecific antibodies targeting PD‑1 and CD22 to overcome adaptive resistance after chronic PD‑1 blockade.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "we built a bispecific antibody where one arm targets CD22, one arm targets PD‑1... we see a remarkable response"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model synergistic signaling effects of dual checkpoint inhibition and prioritize bispecific formats.

crispro_opportunity.description: Use CD22 expression on progenitor exhausted T cells as a biomarker to select patients who may benefit from CD22‑targeted adjunct therapy.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "CD22 is caught our attention for a couple reasons. Very clearly the protein is pretty highly expressed, but only on the progenitor exhausted T cells"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate flow cytometry and transcriptomic data to generate a predictive CD22‑positive Tpex signature.

cited_competitors.name: Nivolumab
cited_competitors.context: Long serum and cell‑surface half‑life limits ability to implement drug holidays

cited_competitors.name: LAG‑3 inhibitors
cited_competitors.context: Mentioned as prior attempts to add checkpoint after PD‑1 progression with modest response rates

unresolved_questions: What is the optimal length of a PD‑1 drug holiday to maximize Tpex recovery without allowing tumor progression?
How does CD22 blockade affect T cell exhaustion in human tumor‑draining lymph nodes?
